pageIntro.definition: Slide a point along one curve, tighten a secant into a tangent, and connect local steepness to the derivative graph without leaving the same live bench.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: At one point, the derivative is the tangent slope: the local rate of change found by shrinking the secant interval.

paragraphs: The derivative stops feeling abstract when the slope question stays attached to one visible point on one visible curve. This module keeps the point, the tangent line, the secant line, and the derivative graph in view together so local rate of change never drifts into detached notation.
The curve here is fixed on purpose. You move the point along it, shrink or widen delta x, and watch the average rate over a secant settle toward the instantaneous slope of the tangent.

keyIdeas: The derivative at one point is the tangent slope there: the local rate of change on the original curve.
A secant slope is an average rate of change across a finite interval, so shrinking delta x moves that average toward the tangent slope.
The derivative graph records those tangent slopes as the point moves along the original curve.

commonMisconception.myth: The derivative is a second graph that has nothing to do with the original curve once the formula is written down.
correction: The derivative graph is built from the slopes of tangent lines on the original curve.

When the tangent is steep and rising, the derivative value is positive and large. When the tangent is flat, the derivative value is near zero. When the tangent falls, the derivative value is negative.

workedExamples.title: Live worked examples
workedExamples.intro: Use the current point and secant on the live curve. The same controls drive the stage, the graph tabs, and these substitutions.

items.title: Compute the secant slope
items.prompt: For the current point and delta x, what average rate of change does the secant line show?

steps.label: 1. Read the two points on the curve

applyAction.label: Use a small delta x
applyAction.observationHint: The secant and tangent slopes move closer together as delta x shrinks.

items.title: Read the tangent slope
items.prompt: At the current point, what does the derivative say the tangent slope is?

steps.label: 1. Identify the point on the curve
3. Connect it to the tangent line
steps.template: That means the tangent line at this point has slope ${{slopeValue}}$.

applyAction.label: Move to a flat point
applyAction.observationHint: Near the turning point, the tangent flattens and the derivative graph comes close to zero.

miniChallenge.prompt: Move the point until the tangent is rising but the secant slope is still noticeably different from the tangent slope.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether you need a larger or smaller delta x before you test it.
miniChallenge.answer: You need a point where the tangent slope is positive and a delta x that is still large enough for the secant to show a visibly different average rate.
miniChallenge.explanation: The tangent slope depends on the local steepness at one point, while the secant slope averages over an interval. Keeping delta x larger preserves that gap.

equations.label: Secant slope
equations.meaning: Measures the average rate of change across a finite horizontal interval.

equations.label: Derivative
equations.meaning: Defines the tangent slope as the limiting value of the secant slope.

equations.label: Tangent line
equations.meaning: Uses the derivative at one point to write the tangent line through that point.

variableLinks.label: Point position
variableLinks.description: Slides the active point along the curve, which changes the point value, the tangent slope, and the derivative-graph reading together.

variableLinks.label: Delta x
variableLinks.description: Sets how far the secant point sits from the main point, which controls how closely the secant slope matches the tangent slope.

variableLinks.label: Show secant
variableLinks.description: Keeps the average-rate comparison visible while you compare it with the tangent slope.

starterExploreTasks: Start with the secant line and notice that its average slope is still visibly different from the tangent.
Move the point along the curve and watch both slopes change together.
Shrink \(\Delta x\) so the secant slope settles toward the tangent slope.
Then open the derivative graph and read that local slope across the whole curve.

controls.label: Point position
controls.description: Move the active point along the curve.

controls.label: Delta x
controls.description: Set the secant-point spacing used for the average rate.

controls.label: Show secant
controls.description: Hide or show the secant comparison line.

presets.label: Negative slope
presets.description: A point where the tangent falls from left to right.

presets.label: Almost flat
presets.description: A point near where the derivative is close to zero.

presets.label: Positive rise
presets.description: A point where the tangent slope is clearly positive.

overlays.label: Tangent line
overlays.shortDescription: Show the instantaneous slope at the current point.
whatToNotice: The tangent touches the curve at one point and matches its local direction there.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the derivative visible as a slope, not just as a symbol.

overlays.label: Secant line
overlays.shortDescription: Show the secant comparison over the current delta x interval.
whatToNotice: A larger delta x keeps the secant slope farther from the tangent slope.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the average-rate idea visible before the limit language takes over.

overlays.label: Delta guide
overlays.shortDescription: Show the horizontal and vertical change used in the difference quotient.
whatToNotice: The secant slope comes from the ratio of the visible vertical and horizontal changes.
overlays.whyItMatters: It links the drawn secant line to the algebra of delta y over delta x.

graphs.label: Derivative graph
graphs.description: Shows how the tangent slope changes across the full curve.

graphs.label: Secant slope vs delta x
graphs.description: Shows how the secant slope approaches the tangent slope as delta x shrinks.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Use the live prompts to keep the geometry and the graph reading tied together.

items.text: Move the point left and right and watch the tangent line switch from falling to rising as the derivative crosses zero.
Shrink delta x and compare the secant slope with the tangent slope. The average rate settles toward the local one.
Use the derivative graph as a slope map: positive values mean the curve rises there, negative values mean it falls there, and zero means a flat tangent.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict the slope behavior before you move the point or shrink delta x.

items.prompt: If you move the point far enough to the right, what should eventually happen to the tangent slope?

choices.label: It should become positive because the curve eventually rises there.
It must stay negative because the curve started with a falling slope.
It should become zero everywhere on the right half.

items.explanation: On the right side of this curve, the tangent eventually tilts upward, so the derivative becomes positive there.
items.observationHint: Watch both the tangent line and the derivative graph at the same time.

items.prompt: If delta x shrinks while the point stays fixed, what should happen to the secant slope?

choices.label: It should move closer to the tangent slope.
It should always move farther away because the interval is smaller.
It should stay exactly the same because the point has not moved.

items.explanation: The derivative is the limit of the difference quotient, so shrinking delta x makes the secant slope approach the tangent slope.
items.observationHint: Compare the secant readout with the tangent readout as the interval tightens.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Use the tangent, secant, and derivative graph together. These checks are about how slope behaves across the curve.

questions.prompt: If the tangent line at a point is horizontal, what must the derivative be there?

choices.label: It must be zero or very close to zero.
It must be positive because the point is still on the curve.

questions.explanation: A horizontal tangent has slope zero, so the derivative value there is zero or nearly zero.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Being on the curve does not force a positive slope. The derivative depends on the tangent direction.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Delta x is an interval size for the secant comparison, not the derivative value itself.

showMeAction.label: Show the nearly flat point
showMeAction.observationHint: The tangent flattens and the derivative graph sits close to the x-axis.

questions.prompt: What is the cleanest difference between a secant slope and a tangent slope?

choices.label: A secant slope averages over an interval, while a tangent slope is local to one point.
A secant slope uses the derivative graph, while a tangent slope does not use the original curve.
They are always identical as long as the curve is smooth.

questions.explanation: The secant uses two points and an interval, while the tangent captures the local slope at one point.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: Both slopes come from the original curve. The derivative graph is built from tangent slopes after the fact.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: They only match closely when delta x is very small.

showMeAction.label: Show a larger secant interval
showMeAction.observationHint: The secant slope is still averaging over a noticeable interval, so it does not yet match the tangent exactly.

questions.prompt: If the derivative graph is below zero at a point, what does that tell you about the original curve there?

choices.label: The original curve is falling from left to right there.
The original curve must be below the x-axis there.
The secant line has been turned off.

questions.explanation: A negative derivative means the tangent slope is negative, so the original curve falls locally from left to right.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: The derivative sign tells you about slope, not the original y-value.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The derivative sign is unrelated to whether the secant overlay is visible.

showMeAction.label: Show a negative slope
showMeAction.observationHint: The tangent falls and the derivative graph sits below zero.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the live point and secant interval as a compact slope bench. The goal is to make the tangent and secant tell the same near-flat story for a real reason, not just to hunt a number.

items.title: Catch the flat tangent
items.prompt: Move to the right-hand turning point so the tangent is almost flat, then shrink delta x until the secant slope is almost flat there too.
items.successMessage: You caught the near-flat point with both the local tangent and the average secant telling the same story. The derivative and difference quotient are now lining up on the same curve behavior.

setup.note: Use the secant graph and the delta guide together while you move toward the right-hand turning point.

hints: The right-hand flat spot sits a little past $x=1.3$.
A smaller delta x is what makes the average rate settle toward the tangent slope.

targets.label: Move the active point into the right-hand flat-turn band between $1.25$ and $1.45$.
Shrink $\Delta x$ into a tight local interval between $0.15$ and $0.3$.
Bring the tangent slope into the near-zero band from $-0.08$ to $0.08$.
Keep the secant slope close too, between $-0.12$ and $0.12$.

paragraphs: A single coordinate grid shows the original curve, one movable point on the curve, an optional secant point, and the tangent line at the main point. Optional guides show the horizontal and vertical changes used in the difference quotient.
Dragging the main point changes the local slope and the derivative reading together. Dragging the secant point changes the interval used for the average rate.
The derivative graph plots tangent slope against x, so it acts as a slope map for the original curve.
The difference-quotient graph plots secant slope against delta x, so it shows how the average rate approaches the tangent slope as the interval shrinks.

seo.title: Derivative as Slope / Local Rate of Change
seo.description: Explore tangent slope, secant slope, and local rate of change with a live curve, derivative graph, and difference-quotient view.

v2.intuition: Slide one point along one curve so secant slope, tangent slope, and the derivative graph stay connected on the same bench.
v2.whyItMatters: This is the first reusable picture of derivative as local steepness, so the bench should make the limit idea feel like a visible refinement, not a notation jump.

guidedSteps.title: Read a secant first
guidedSteps.goal: Treat the secant line as an average slope before you ask for a derivative.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start with the secant line visible, keep the difference-quotient graph open, and move the point once.
guidedSteps.notice: The secant follows the local trend, but its slope still depends on the second point separated by delta x.
guidedSteps.explain: A secant slope is an average rate of change across an interval, so it is close to the derivative without yet being the tangent slope.

guidedSteps.title: Shrink delta x
guidedSteps.goal: Watch the secant settle toward the tangent as the two points come together.
guidedSteps.doThis: Reduce Delta x until the secant almost sits on top of the tangent line.
guidedSteps.notice: The difference-quotient value stabilizes toward the tangent slope as the interval becomes very small.
guidedSteps.explain: The derivative is the limit of the difference quotient as delta x approaches zero, so tightening the interval reveals the local rate.

guidedSteps.title: Read the derivative graph
guidedSteps.goal: Use the derivative graph as a map of local steepness along the original curve.
guidedSteps.doThis: Move the point to the flat-turn preset and then to a rising section while the derivative graph stays visible.
guidedSteps.notice: Where the tangent turns flat, the derivative graph moves toward zero; where the tangent rises, the derivative graph becomes positive.
guidedSteps.explain: The derivative graph records the tangent slope you would read from the original curve at each x-position.

inlineCheck.title: Can you find a flat tangent?
inlineCheck.note: Move the point until the tangent is horizontal but the curve is still clearly changing shape around it.

learned: The derivative at one point is the tangent slope there: the local rate of change on the original curve.
Shrinking delta x makes the secant slope approach that tangent slope.
The derivative graph stores the local tangent slope across the whole curve.
wrapUp.misconception: The derivative is just another average slope between two nearby points.

nextConcepts.title: Limits and Continuity / Approaching a Value
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Support the limit picture underneath derivative

nextConcepts.title: Integral as Accumulation / Area
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Pair local rate with accumulated total

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Find one x-position where the derivative is negative, one where it is zero, and one where it is positive.
